meta.title: How does this all-female species reproduce?
meta.speaker: Susan Freitas and Darren Parker
meta.youtubeTitle: How does this all-female species reproduce? - Susana Freitas and Darren Parker

sentences.text: In 2021, workers at a Sardinian aquarium were stunned by the birth of a smoothhound shark, who they called Ispera.
sentences.translation: 2021 年，撒丁岛一家水族馆的 工作人员惊奇地发现一条星鲨出生了， 并给它起名为 Ispera。

sentences.text: What shocked them was that, for the last decade, Ispera’s mother had been living only with other females.
sentences.translation: 令人震惊的是，过去十年里，Ispera 的母亲一直只和 其它雌性鲨鱼生活在一起。

sentences.text: But it’s actually entirely possible that Ispera had no father— and the reason why that is also explains other biological curiosities, like the existence of an all-female lizard species.
sentences.translation: 但 Ispera 没有父亲是完全有可能的，而其背后的原因也可以 解释其它奇特的生物，比如全雌性的蜥蜴物种的存在。

sentences.text: Usually sexual species have sex cells that contain half the number of chromosomes required to create a viable embryo.
sentences.translation: 有性繁殖的物种通常有性细胞，包含形成胚胎所需的一半染色体。

sentences.text: So an egg cell must be fertilized by a sperm cell to form two full sets of chromosomes.
sentences.translation: 因此，卵细胞必须由精子受精才能形成两套完整的染色体。

sentences.text: But some species that have sex cells can undergo a type of asexual reproduction called parthenogenesis— meaning “virgin origin” in Greek.
sentences.translation: 不过，一些具有性细胞的物种可以进行一种无性繁殖， 即“孤雌生殖” (parthenogenesis)，在希腊语中是“处女起源”的意思。

sentences.text: In parthenogenesis, an embryo develops from an unfertilized egg cell that doubles its own chromosome count.
sentences.translation: 在孤雌生殖中， 胚胎由未受精的卵细胞在染色体数目翻倍后发育而来。

sentences.text: In fact, some animals only ever undergo parthenogenesis, while others can reproduce both sexually and parthenogenetically.
sentences.translation: 实际上，有些动物只进行孤雌生殖，而另些则既可以进行有性繁殖， 也能进行孤雌生殖。

sentences.text: It's actually more common than previously thought.
sentences.translation: 这种现象比先前认为的更常见。

sentences.text: More than 80 different sexual vertebrate species— including Komodo dragons and certain kinds of turkeys, pythons, and sharks— have surprised us by occasionally reproducing this way.
sentences.translation: 超过80种不同的有性脊椎动物，包括科摩多巨蜥和 一些火鸡、蟒蛇、鲨鱼等，偶尔会以这种令人惊讶的方式繁殖。

sentences.text: These discoveries were usually made when females unexpectedly gave birth in captivity.
sentences.translation: 这些现象往往是通过被捕的雌性动物意外分娩发现的。

sentences.text: Ispera’s birth, for one, may have been the first account of parthenogenesis in smoothhound sharks.
sentences.translation: Ispera 的诞生则是记录中的第一例星鲨孤雌生殖。

sentences.text: Scientists also confirmed that parthenogenesis was taking place in some wild snake populations.
sentences.translation: 科学家们也发现，孤雌生殖还会在某些野生蛇类种群中发生。

sentences.text: But just how many fatherless creatures are running, slithering, and swimming around out there is unknown: it’s a tough thing to track without population-wide genetic analyses.
sentences.translation: 但是，没有人知道 世界上到底有多少无父亲的生物在四处游走：由于缺乏种群范围内的遗传分析， 追踪变得非常困难。

sentences.text: So, why is it happening at all?
sentences.translation: 那么， 这到底为什么发生呢？

sentences.text: Scientists think parthenogenesis could be evolutionarily beneficial in some contexts because, well, sex can be a drag.
sentences.translation: 科学家们认为，孤雌生殖有时候 在进化上更有利，因为交配可以成为一种负担。

sentences.text: Mating and its associated demands and rituals can be time- and energy-intensive, leave individuals vulnerable to predators, and even be fatal.
sentences.translation: 交配和其相关的需求规范 需要大量的时间和能量，使个体易受到捕食者的伤害， 甚至会致命。

sentences.text: Parthenogenesis, meanwhile, requires only one parent.
sentences.translation: 而孤雌生殖只需要一位父母。

sentences.text: Mayflies can sometimes default to parthenogenesis if there are no males available, which is especially handy because they’ve only got a day or so to reproduce before dying.
sentences.translation: 蜉蝣在没有雄性存在的条件下可以转为孤雌生殖，这非常方便，因为它们在死亡前只有一天左右的时间进行繁殖。

sentences.text: It can also help rapidly expand a population.
sentences.translation: 这也能帮助迅速扩大种群。

sentences.text: In the summer, when food is abundant, pea aphids can rely on parthenogenesis, allowing their population to explode under favorable conditions.
sentences.translation: 在夏季食物充足的时候，豌豆蚜可以孤雌生殖，让种群数量在有利环境中迅速膨胀。

sentences.text: And in the autumn, they switch back to sex.
sentences.translation: 到了秋季，它们就会回归有性生殖。

sentences.text: But some aphids, katydids, lizards, geckos, and snakes only ever reproduce via parthenogenesis.
sentences.translation: 而有些蚜虫、蝈蝈、 蜥蜴、壁虎、蛇等只进行孤雌生殖。

sentences.text: So, why do other animals bother with sex?
sentences.translation: 那么，为什么还需要有性生殖呢？

sentences.text: Scientists hypothesize that sex makes up for its shortcomings with long-term gains.
sentences.translation: 科学家们提出假说， 认为有性生殖的长期收益能弥补不足。

sentences.text: It allows individuals to mix their genes, leading to greater genetic diversity.
sentences.translation: 有性繁殖能混合不同个体的基因， 从而提高遗传多样性。

sentences.text: That way, when the going gets tough, beneficial mutations can be selected and harmful ones can be removed without ending the entire population.
sentences.translation: 这样，当环境变得不利于生存时，自然选择可以保留有益突变、剔除有害突变，而不至于让整个种群灭绝。

sentences.text: In a parthenogenetic population, on the other hand, individuals can only reproduce using their own genetic material.
sentences.translation: 另一方面，在孤雌生殖的群体中，个体只能利用自身的遗传物质进行繁殖。

sentences.text: According to a theory called Muller’s ratchet, that’s not good.
sentences.translation: 根据穆勒棘轮效应，这不是好事。

sentences.text: The theory predicts that parthenogenetic lineages will accumulate harmful mutations over time and eventually, after thousands of generations, will reach a point of so-called mutational meltdown.
sentences.translation: 此理论推测，孤雌生殖的血族 会逐渐积累有害突变，最终在几千代以后，达到所谓的“变异熔断”。

sentences.text: At this stage, individuals will be so compromised that they can't reproduce, so the population will nosedive, leading to extinction.
sentences.translation: 到这个节点，个体已经非常受损， 无法再继续繁殖，种群数量将猛跌造成灭绝。

sentences.text: We haven’t yet seen this entire process unfold in nature.
sentences.translation: 我们还没在自然界中 观察到整个过程发生，

sentences.text: But scientists have observed an accumulation of harmful mutations in parthenogenetic stick insects that are absent in their sexual relatives.
sentences.translation: 但科学家们已经发现， 一种孤雌生殖的粘虫在缺乏性伴侣时 积累起有害的变异。

sentences.text: Only time will tell whether this will cause their extinction.
sentences.translation: 只有时间才能证明， 这是否会导致它们的灭绝。

sentences.text: Otherwise, some parthenogenetic species appear to have ways of circumventing a mutational meltdown.
sentences.translation: 不过，一些孤雌生殖的物种 似乎有策略规避变异熔断。

sentences.text: New Mexico whiptail lizards came about when two different lizard species hybridized, creating this new all-female species.
sentences.translation: 新墨西哥鞭尾蜥是通过两种不同蜥蜴的杂交形成，是一种新的全雌性物种。

sentences.text: As hybrids, their genome is a combination of the different sets of chromosomes from their two parent species.
sentences.translation: 作为杂交种，它们的基因组分别是由两个亲代物种的染色体组合而成。

sentences.text: This gives them a high level of genetic diversity, which may allow them to survive long into the future.
sentences.translation: 这使它们具有高度的遗传多样性，也许有助于未来的长期生存。

sentences.text: Bdelloid rotifers, meanwhile, have been reproducing parthenogenetically for 60 million years.
sentences.translation: 与此同时，蛭形轮虫已经孤雌生殖了近 6000 万年。

sentences.text: They might have managed this by taking in foreign genetic material.
sentences.translation: 它们可能是通过吸收外来的遗传物质 以避免变异熔断。

sentences.text: Indeed, about 10% of their genes comes from other organisms, like fungi, bacteria, and algae.
sentences.translation: 实际上，它们大概 10% 的 基因来自其它生物，像真菌、细菌、藻类等。

sentences.text: How exactly they do this is unclear, but whatever the trick is, it seems to be working.
sentences.translation: 这其中的过程无人了解，但不论如何，这种方法似乎有作用。

sentences.text: To totally untangle the mysteries of reproduction, we’ll need more research— and probably a few more surprises like Ispera.
sentences.translation: 为了完全揭开繁殖的奥秘，我们需要更多研究——以及像 Ispera 这样的意外。

cues.text: In 2021, workers at a Sardinian aquarium were stunned by the birth
of a smoothhound shark, who they called Ispera.
What shocked them was that, for the last decade,
Ispera’s mother had been living only with other females.
But it’s actually entirely possible that Ispera had no father—
and the reason why that is also explains other biological curiosities,
like the existence of an all-female lizard species.
Usually sexual species have sex cells that contain
half the number of chromosomes required to create a viable embryo.
So an egg cell must be fertilized by a sperm cell
to form two full sets of chromosomes.
But some species that have sex cells can undergo
a type of asexual reproduction called parthenogenesis—
meaning “virgin origin” in Greek.
In parthenogenesis, an embryo develops from an unfertilized egg cell
that doubles its own chromosome count.
In fact, some animals only ever undergo parthenogenesis,
while others can reproduce both sexually and parthenogenetically.
It's actually more common than previously thought.
More than 80 different sexual vertebrate species—
including Komodo dragons and certain kinds of turkeys, pythons, and sharks—
have surprised us by occasionally reproducing this way.
These discoveries were usually made when females
unexpectedly gave birth in captivity.
Ispera’s birth, for one, may have been the first account
of parthenogenesis in smoothhound sharks.
Scientists also confirmed that parthenogenesis was taking place
in some wild snake populations.
But just how many fatherless creatures are running, slithering, and swimming
around out there is unknown:
it’s a tough thing to track without population-wide genetic analyses.
So, why is it happening at all?
Scientists think parthenogenesis could be evolutionarily beneficial
in some contexts because, well, sex can be a drag.
Mating and its associated demands and rituals can be time- and energy-intensive,
leave individuals vulnerable to predators, and even be fatal.
Parthenogenesis, meanwhile, requires only one parent.
Mayflies can sometimes default to parthenogenesis
if there are no males available,
which is especially handy because they’ve only got a day or so
to reproduce before dying.
It can also help rapidly expand a population.
In the summer, when food is abundant,
pea aphids can rely on parthenogenesis,
allowing their population to explode under favorable conditions.
And in the autumn, they switch back to sex.
But some aphids, katydids, lizards, geckos, and snakes
only ever reproduce via parthenogenesis.
So, why do other animals bother with sex?
Scientists hypothesize that sex makes up for its shortcomings with long-term gains.
It allows individuals to mix their genes, leading to greater genetic diversity.
That way, when the going gets tough,
beneficial mutations can be selected and harmful ones can be removed
without ending the entire population.
In a parthenogenetic population, on the other hand,
individuals can only reproduce using their own genetic material.
According to a theory called Muller’s ratchet,
that’s not good.
The theory predicts that parthenogenetic lineages will accumulate harmful mutations
over time and eventually, after thousands of generations,
will reach a point of so-called mutational meltdown.
At this stage, individuals will be so compromised that they can't reproduce,
so the population will nosedive, leading to extinction.
We haven’t yet seen this entire process unfold in nature.
But scientists have observed an accumulation of harmful mutations
in parthenogenetic stick insects that are absent in their sexual relatives.
Only time will tell whether this will cause their extinction.
Otherwise, some parthenogenetic species appear to have ways of circumventing
a mutational meltdown.
New Mexico whiptail lizards came about
when two different lizard species hybridized,
creating this new all-female species.
As hybrids, their genome is a combination of the different sets of chromosomes
from their two parent species.
This gives them a high level of genetic diversity,
which may allow them to survive long into the future.
Bdelloid rotifers, meanwhile, have been reproducing parthenogenetically
for 60 million years.
They might have managed this by taking in foreign genetic material.
Indeed, about 10% of their genes comes from other organisms,
like fungi, bacteria, and algae.
How exactly they do this is unclear,
but whatever the trick is, it seems to be working.
To totally untangle the mysteries of reproduction,
we’ll need more research—
and probably a few more surprises like Ispera.

cuesZh.text: 2021 年，撒丁岛一家水族馆的 工作人员惊奇地发现
一条星鲨出生了， 并给它起名为 Ispera。
令人震惊的是，过去十年里，
Ispera 的母亲一直只和 其它雌性鲨鱼生活在一起。
但 Ispera 没有父亲是完全有可能的，
而其背后的原因也可以 解释其它奇特的生物，
比如全雌性的蜥蜴物种的存在。
有性繁殖的物种通常有性细胞，
包含形成胚胎所需的一半染色体。
因此，卵细胞必须由精子受精
才能形成两套完整的染色体。
不过，一些具有性细胞的物种
可以进行一种无性繁殖， 即“孤雌生殖” (parthenogenesis)，
在希腊语中是“处女起源”的意思。
在孤雌生殖中， 胚胎由未受精的卵细胞
在染色体数目翻倍后发育而来。
实际上，有些动物只进行孤雌生殖，
而另些则既可以进行有性繁殖， 也能进行孤雌生殖。
这种现象比先前认为的更常见。
超过80种不同的有性脊椎动物，
包括科摩多巨蜥和 一些火鸡、蟒蛇、鲨鱼等，
偶尔会以这种令人惊讶的方式繁殖。
这些现象往往是通过被捕的雌性动物
意外分娩发现的。
Ispera 的诞生则是记录中的
第一例星鲨孤雌生殖。
科学家们也发现，孤雌生殖还会
在某些野生蛇类种群中发生。
但是，没有人知道 世界上到底有多少
无父亲的生物在四处游走：
由于缺乏种群范围内的遗传分析， 追踪变得非常困难。
那么， 这到底为什么发生呢？
科学家们认为，孤雌生殖有时候 在进化上更有利，
因为交配可以成为一种负担。
交配和其相关的需求规范 需要大量的时间和能量，
使个体易受到捕食者的伤害， 甚至会致命。
而孤雌生殖只需要一位父母。
蜉蝣在没有雄性存在的条件下
可以转为孤雌生殖，
这非常方便，因为它们在死亡前
只有一天左右的时间进行繁殖。
这也能帮助迅速扩大种群。
在夏季食物充足的时候，
豌豆蚜可以孤雌生殖，
让种群数量在有利环境中迅速膨胀。
到了秋季，它们就会回归有性生殖。
而有些蚜虫、蝈蝈、 蜥蜴、壁虎、蛇等
只进行孤雌生殖。
那么，为什么还需要有性生殖呢？
科学家们提出假说， 认为有性生殖的长期收益能弥补不足。
有性繁殖能混合不同个体的基因， 从而提高遗传多样性。
这样，当环境变得不利于生存时，
自然选择可以保留有益突变、剔除有害突变，
而不至于让整个种群灭绝。
另一方面，在孤雌生殖的群体中，
个体只能利用自身的遗传物质进行繁殖。
根据穆勒棘轮效应，
这不是好事。
此理论推测，孤雌生殖的血族 会逐渐积累有害突变，
最终在几千代以后，
达到所谓的“变异熔断”。
到这个节点，个体已经非常受损， 无法再继续繁殖，
种群数量将猛跌造成灭绝。
我们还没在自然界中 观察到整个过程发生，
但科学家们已经发现， 一种孤雌生殖的粘虫
在缺乏性伴侣时 积累起有害的变异。
只有时间才能证明， 这是否会导致它们的灭绝。
不过，一些孤雌生殖的物种 似乎有策略规避
变异熔断。
新墨西哥鞭尾蜥是通过
两种不同蜥蜴的杂交形成，
是一种新的全雌性物种。
作为杂交种，它们的基因组分别是由两个
亲代物种的染色体组合而成。
这使它们具有高度的遗传多样性，
也许有助于未来的长期生存。
与此同时，蛭形轮虫已经孤雌生殖了
它们可能是通过吸收外来的遗传物质 以避免变异熔断。
实际上，它们大概 10% 的 基因来自其它生物，
像真菌、细菌、藻类等。
这其中的过程无人了解，
但不论如何，这种方法似乎有作用。
为了完全揭开繁殖的奥秘，
我们需要更多研究——
以及像 Ispera 这样的意外。
